LAW AND MOTION DEPT. 302: CROSS-DEFENDANT DEVIN REDMOND'S DEMURRER TO CROSS-COMPLAINT IS OVERRULED. THE CROSS-COMPLAINT ALLEGES THAT DEVIN BREACHED THE IMPLIED COVENANT OF GOOD FAITH AND FAIR DEALING IN THE PARTIES' SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT BY, INTER ALIA, FILING THE INSTANT ACTION. THE CLAIM DOES NOT MERELY RESTATE A BREACH OF AN EXPRESS TERM OF THE AGREEMENT, NOR DOES IT SEEK TO OBLITERATE A RIGHT EXPRESSLY PROVIDED UNDER THE AGREEMENT. (SEE CAREAU & CO. V. SECURITY PACIFIC BUSINESS CREDIT, INC. (1990) 222 CAL.APP.3D 1371, 1395 [COURT MAY DISREGARD AS SUPERFLUOUS BREACH OF IMPLIED COVENANT CLAIM THAT MERELY RESTATES A BREACH OF AN EXPRESS TERM]; GERDLUND V. ELECTRONIC DISPENSERS INT'L (1987) 190 CAL.APP.3D 263, 277 [IMPLIED COVENANT CANNOT BE USED TO OVERRIDE EXPRESS TERM].) THEREFORE, THE DEMURRER IS OVERRULED. LEA'S REQUEST FOR FEES/SANCTIONS IS DENIED. DEVIN'S ARGUMENT-WHILE UNTIMELY-WAS NOT FRIVOLOUS. WRITTEN ORDER IS SIGNED. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: J. RIOS MERIDA; REPORTER: PROCEEDINGS NOT REPORTED.

DEMURRER to CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-05-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance is made in this matter.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

CROSS-DEFENDANT DEVIN REDMOND's DEMURRER to CROSS-COMPLAINT is OVERRULED. The cross-complaint alleges that Devin breached the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in the parties' settlement agreement by, inter alia, filing the instant action. The claim does not merely restate a breach of an express term of the agreement, nor does it seek to obliterate a right expressly provided under the agreement. (See Careau & Co. v. Security Pacific Business Credit, Inc. (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 1371, 1395 [court may disregard as superfluous breach of implied covenant claim that merely restates a breach of an express term]; Gerdlund v. Electronic Dispensers Int'l (1987) 190 Cal.App.3d 263, 277 [implied covenant cannot be used to override express term].) Therefore, the demurrer is overruled. Lea's request for fees/sanctions is denied. Devin's argument-while untimely-was not frivolous. Written order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Proceedings not reported.

Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Lea Redmond To Intervene; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities - **Redacted**
Off Calendar Jun-18-2020 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. JOHN M. BEGAKIS, Esq. of altView Law Group LLP (Tel: 310-663-7638) appearing via CourtCall for plaintiff Devin Redmond;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: PROSPECTIVE INTERVENOR'S LEA REDMOND'S Motion To Intervene.

The Court indicates that plaintiff's counsel did not properly notify the Court of his intent to contest the Court's tentative ruling. As counsel for the opposing side is not present, the Court indicates it will not hear argument from plaintiff.

The Court adopts its tentative ruling: The motion of Lea Redmon to intervene in this action as a matter of right under Code of Civil Procedure section 387(d)(1) is granted. A person has an unconditional right to intervene in an action if, upon timely application, she "claims an interest relating to the property or transaction that is the subject of the action," (2) she "is so situated that the disposition of the action may impair or impede [her] ability to protect that interest," and (3) her interest is not "adequately represented by one or more of the existing parties." (Code Civ. Proc. 387(d)(1); see Mylan Laboratories Inc. v. Soon-Shiong (1999) 76 Cal.App.4th 71, 77-78.) The action having been recently filed on March 2, 2020, the motion is timely. All three factors are met. Plaintiff alleges in the complaint that Ms. Redmon breached the publishing agreements by requesting defendant Chronicle Books, LLC to forward all future payments on those agreements directly to her individually, rather than to Leafcutter Designs LLC, and that Chronicle wrongfully complied with that request. (Compl. 11, 12.) Ms. Redmon claims an interest in the publishing agreements and royalty payments that are the subject of the action, and specifically contends that plaintiff's claims are barred, among other things, by the settlement agreement between her and plaintiff. (E.g., L. Redmon Decl. 2-11 & Exs. A, B; [Proposed] Answer in Intervention, Third Affirmative Defense; [Proposed] Verified Cross-Complaint 4 [alleging that plaintiff "seeks to sidestep the terms of the settlement agreement and double-dip into Lea's royalty payments"].) Further, Ms. Redmon also is contractually required to indemnify defendant. Allowing plaintiff to prosecute the action in Ms. Redmon's absence may impair or impede her ability to protect those interests. Finally, it is apparent from the parties' disagreement regarding the effect, if any, of their settlement agreement on plaintiff's claims that Ms. Redmon's interest is not adequately represented by plaintiff.

In the alternative, Ms. Redmon is entitled at a minimum to permissive intervention pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 387(d)(2). A trial court has "broad discretion" in determining whether to permit intervention. (City of Malibu v. California Coastal Comm. (2005) 128 Cal.App.4th 897, 902.) Permitting intervention will conserve judicial resources and allow for efficient resolution of all related issues in a single action. When Ms. Redmon appears as a party by filing her proposed answer in intervention, she may file a cross-complaint against plaintiff. (Code Civ. Proc. 428.50(a).)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
